---
title: "Prescriptive Assignment 3 Report"
author: "Rick de Rijk, Tycho van Rooij, Stefan Vonk & Garoe"
subtitle: "Prediction & Predictive coming together"
format:
  pdf:
    include-in-header:
      text: |
        \usepackage{enumitem}
        \setlist[itemize]{itemsep=2pt, topsep=2pt, parsep=0pt, partopsep=0pt}
        \setlist[enumerate]{itemsep=2pt, topsep=2pt, parsep=0pt, partopsep=0pt}
        \usepackage{setspace}
        \setstretch{0.96}
        \usepackage{parskip}
        \setlength{\parskip}{0.3em}
        \setlength{\parindent}{0pt}
geometry:
      - margin=2cm
editor: visual
toc: true
lof: true
lot: true
number-sections: true
---

\newpage

# Introduction

Company X operates a fleet of 100 aircraft engines subject to wear and tear over time. To avoid unsafe operation and minimise costs, engines must be maintained before they fail. A key quantity for planning this maintenance is the Remaining Useful Lifetime (RUL): the number of cycles an engine can still operate before failure. This report addresses two tasks: first, developing a predictive model to estimate the RUL of engines currently in service; second, using those predictions as input to a Genetic Algorithm that constructs an optimal maintenance schedule. This report covers the Prediction Task in full.

# Prediction Task

## Data Analysis and Feature Engineering

**Dataset.** The training dataset (DataTrain) contains run-to-failure observations for 100 engines across 20,631 rows, with 21 sensor measurements and 3 operational settings recorded per cycle. Engines begin with varying degrees of initial wear and are recorded until failure.

**Constructing RUL.** The dataset does not include an explicit RUL column. Since each engine's final recorded cycle corresponds to failure, the RUL at any earlier cycle is the number of cycles remaining until that point. This yields:

```         
RUL = max_cycle(engine_id) − cycle 
```

Applied row-wise using a grouped transform, this produces RUL values ranging from 0 (failure) to 361 cycles, with a mean of approximately 108 cycles. The cycle index itself is not used as a model feature: an engine at cycle 50 may have very different RUL depending on its total lifespan, making the raw cycle value non-transferable across engines.

**Exploratory Data Analysis.** Variance was computed across all sensors and settings to identify informative features. Ten sensors and all three operational settings exhibited near-zero variance (\< 0.01), meaning their values remained essentially constant across the entire engine lifecycle and carry no predictive signal @fig-variance . The flat line behaviour of these sensors is illustrated in @fig-flat-sensors. These were flagged for removal. The remaining sensors were plotted against RUL for a sample of engines, confirming systematic monotonic trends: sensors such as `sensor_val19` and `sensor_val10` increase as RUL decreases, while `sensor_val2` and `sensor_val5` decrease Confirmed by plotting sensor values against RUL for sample of three engines @fig-sensor-trends-rul. Pearson correlation analysis confirmed these observations, with six sensors exceeding \|r\| = 0.5 with RUL @fig-pearson. A correlation heatmap further revealed substantial multicollinearity among retained sensors, notably `sensor_val1` and `sensor_val3` (r = 0.96) and `sensor_val2` and `sensor_val5` (r = 0.81), indicating redundancy in the raw feature set @fig-heatmap.

**Feature Engineering.** All 12 near-constant features identified in the variance analysis were dropped (Figures 1–2). The remaining 12 sensors were retained as raw features. Additionally, 10-cycle rolling means and standard deviations were computed per engine for each retained sensor: the rolling mean captures the local degradation trend rather than the noisy instantaneous reading, while the rolling std captures volatility as a secondary degradation signal. The final feature set consists of 36 features: 12 raw sensors, 12 rolling means, and 12 rolling standard deviations.

## Model Development

**Data splitting.** The 100 training engines were split into 80 training engines and 20 held-out test engines (80/20). The split is performed at the engine level rather than the row level to prevent data leakage: rows from the same engine are temporally correlated, so a row-level split would allow the model to observe earlier cycles of an engine during training and later cycles of the same engine at test time. The 20 test engines were never seen during training and constitute a genuine out-of-sample evaluation. The model was configured with these parameters that can been seen in table 1

The combination of row and feature subsampling implements stochastic gradient boosting, which is particularly beneficial given the multicollinearity in the feature set.

**Validation.** Five-fold cross-validation was applied to the 80 training engines using `GroupKFold` with `engine_id` as the grouping variable, ensuring all rows of a given engine remain within a single fold. Standard `KFold` would mix rows from the same engine across folds, reintroducing the leakage problem. Cross-validation was scored using RMSE.

**Evaluation metrics.** RMSE is the primary metric. It is expressed in cycles, making it directly interpretable as average prediction error, and penalises large errors more heavily, appropriate since severely underestimating RUL could result in missed maintenance and safety penalties. MAE is reported as a complementary metric, treating all errors equally and representing the typical prediction error. R² measures the proportion of variance in RUL explained by the model.

## Prediction Results

The model was retrained on all 100 training engines before predicting on DataSchedule, maximising the available training information. The identical feature engineering pipeline was applied to DataSchedule. Since DataSchedule does not contain run-to-failure sequences, predictions were made at the last recorded cycle per engine, representing the engine's current state. All predictions were rounded to integer values and clipped at zero.

Cross-validation yielded a mean RMSE of **42.94 ± 3.30 cycles** across five folds, with individual fold scores ranging from 38.65 to 46.34. The low standard deviation confirms stable performance across different engine subsets. The held-out test set yielded an RMSE of **45.85 cycles**, MAE of **33.27 cycles**, and R² of **0.593**. The proximity of the test RMSE to the cross-validation mean (difference of \~3 cycles) confirms that no overfitting occurred, validating the engine-level GroupKFold setup and regularisation choices. The model performs best at low RUL values, the operationally critical region for maintenance scheduling and shows increasing prediction error at high RUL, attributable to the relative scarcity of high-RUL observations in run-to-failure training data. The scatter and residual plots confirm these findings @fig-prediction

Across the 100 DataSchedule engines, predicted RUL values range from 8 to 219 cycles, with a mean of **94.9 cycles** and a standard deviation of 52.9 cycles.

## Comparison with Consultancy Predictions

The consultancy's predictions yield a mean of 82.9 cycles (std = 48.4), compared to our mean of 94.9 cycles, a difference of 12.0 cycles with an MAE of 21.45 cycles between the two sets. Both a Wilcoxon signed-rank test (stat = 1187.50, p \< 0.0001) and a paired t-test (stat = 4.43, p \< 0.0001) confirm this difference is statistically significant. The comparison plots @fig-box-hist-scat show broad agreement at low RUL values with divergence increasing at higher values, a right-skewed difference distribution centred at 12.0 cycles, and a wider interquartile range in our predictions reflecting greater individual spread.

However, this result must be interpreted carefully. Our test RMSE of 45.85 cycles represents an inherent error range that comfortably exceeds the 12.0-cycle offset, meaning we cannot claim our predictions are closer to the true RUL values than the consultancy's. The statistically significant difference confirms the two sets are distinct, but the practical significance is better assessed through the downstream maintenance schedule, examined in the Optimization Task. The systematic positive offset is consistent with the high-RUL under representation bias identified during evaluation, and practically results in fewer engines falling within the 30-day planning horizon compared to the consultancy's more conservative estimates.

# Optimization Task

## Problem Formulation

-   Description of maintenance scheduling problem\
-   Constraints\
-   Objective: minimize penalty costs

## Genetic Algorithm Design

### Representation

Individuals in our implementation are represented using a list of tuples, where each tuple corresponds to a maintained engine and contains three elements: the ID of the engine, which maintenance team is assigned to it, and the day at which the maintenance starts. There is technically no upper limit to the number of tuples in the list, but a minimum of 1 is maintained during mutation to ensure an individual's schedule cannot become empty. This representation allows for flexible scheduling of any subset of engines across the 30-day planning horizon, while also encoding the necessary information for evaluating maintenance costs and constraints. It also inherently enforces the constraint that only one maintenance team can work on an engine at a time, as only one team id can be present in an engine's tuple.

### Selection Strategy

-   Method used

### Crossover

We make use of a custom crossover method designed to give a high probability that the schedules of the offspring are still valid schedules. After all, if we used a standard approach such as single-point crossover, there is a high chance that the insertion of an engine from parent B into schedule A would cause some form of conflict or overlap with the existing schedule, making at worst the entire schedule after the insertion invalid. Instead, our method has each child inherit half of each parent's schedule. This way, at least half of the schedule is guaranteed to be valid, and the other half is at the very least internally valid. The only remaining point in the schedule where a conflict could arise is at the point where the two halves are joined, but the repair function (see "Feasibility Handling") is designed to resolve any such conflicts. Before finishing the crossover, there is a check for any duplicate engines in the offspring's schedules. If there is, the later occurrence of the duplicate engine is removed from the schedule, ensuring that each engine is only maintained once.

### Mutation

For mutation, we implement a multi-stage mutation process that ensures every part of the schedule that can be mutated has a chance to be mutated. This way, we ensure that the search space is fully explored and reduce the chance of getting stuck in a local minimum. The implementation applies a chance to mutate on every chromosome (tuple) in the individual; to make sure we do not mutate individuals too much (which could result in losing otherwise good solutions), the base mutation probability is kept relatively low (0.3).
The first stage, if activated, mutates the starting day of maintenance within a reasonable range (+/- 3 days). While we do want to vary the start dates, changing the dates by too much risks creating many overlaps and otherwise infeasible schedules. A range of 3 days in either direction gives a good balance of schedule flexibility and validity.
Next, there is a chance to mutate which team maintains the engine. This very simply randomly selects one of the four available teams, and replaces the currently set team with whichever one is selected.
Following that, there is also a chance to mutate the engine ID, which works similarly to the team mutation. In both cases, there is a chance to re-select the same team or engine (1/4 and 1/100 chance respectively). This is not a major issue, as the chances for this to happen are not very high (especially in the engine ID case). Additionally, if it should happen, then subsequent mutations either in the same round or in the next round of mutation will make up for it either way.
The final mutation stage changes the individuals' chromosome length (amount of tuples). The standard probability (0.3) is used to decide to remove a chromosome, with a check in place to ensure
this only happens if the individual has more than one chromosome to begin with. After that, the inverse probability (0.7) is used to decide whether to add a chromosome. If this happens, a new random tuple is added with the same random value ranges used during the initalization of individuals. The probability used here is higher, because we want to encourage scheduling as many
engines as possible within the constraints as this should lead to the highest objective value; after all, non-maintained engines incurr a significant penalty.

### Feasibility Handling

-   How infeasible solutions are handled

### Hyperparameters

-   Population size\
-   Crossover probability\
-   Mutation probability\
-   Other design choices

## Optimization Results

-   Best solution found\
-   Description of maintenance schedule\
-   Total penalty cost

## Performance Analysis

-   Description of 30 runs experiment\
-   Average-best-fitness plot\
-   Interpretation of results

## Comparison with Consultancy RUL

-   Results using consultancy predictions\
-   Comparison with own predictions\
-   Explanation of differences

# Discussion

-   Key insights\
-   Strengths of the approach\
-   Limitations

# Conclusion

-   Summary of findings\
-   Final remarks

# References

-   Sources used

\newpage

# Appendix

| Hyperparameter | Value | Justification |
|------------------------|------------------------|------------------------|
| `n_estimators` | 400 | Sufficient rounds to converge at the chosen learning rate |
| `max_depth` | 4 | Shallow trees reduce overfitting on a \~16,000-row training set |
| `learning_rate` | 0.05 | Conservative step size paired with 400 estimators |
| `subsample` | 0.8 | Row subsampling per tree; reduces variance between trees |
| `colsample_bytree` | 0.8 | Feature subsampling; reduces correlation between trees |
| `reg_alpha` | 0.1 | L1 regularisation; encourages sparsity |
| `reg_lambda` | 2 | L2 regularisation; shrinks leaf weights against overfitting |
| `min_child_weight` | 3 | Prevents splits on very small data subsets |

: Hyperparameter tuning

![variance Bar chart](images/plot1_variance.png){#fig-variance}

![flat-sensors](images/plot3_flat_sensors.png){#fig-flat-sensors}

![Sensor trends VS RUL](images/plot2_sensor_trends.png){#fig-sensor-trends-rul}

![Pearson Correlation](images/plot4_correlation_with_RUL.png){#fig-pearson}

![Heatmap](images/plot5_heatmap.png){#fig-heatmap}

![Predicted vs actual + residuals](images/plot_model_evaluation.png){#fig-prediction}

![Histogram-scatter-boxplot](images/plot_consultancy_comparison.png){#fig-box-hist-scat}

#
